name: Sardis Artemis
description: The Temple of Artemis at Sardis, located in the ancient Lydian capital, was one of the largest and most impressive Ionic temples in the ancient world. Its construction began in the Hellenistic period, likely in the 3rd century BCE, and continued, with interruptions and modifications, for centuries. Dedicated to Artemis, the Greek equivalent of the Lydian goddess Artimus (also associated with Cybele), the temple served as a central point of worship and a symbol of Lydian religious identity. The temple's massive columns and elaborate architectural details reflected the wealth and power of Sardis. Over time, the temple underwent syncretic changes, incorporating elements of Roman imperial cult worship alongside the traditional Artemis cult, demonstrating the adaptability of religious practices in the region. Its ruins stand today as a testament to the rich cultural and religious history of Lydia, bridging Greek, Roman, and Anatolian traditions. The site's importance extended beyond religious practice, acting as a social and economic hub for the surrounding community. Archaeological excavations have revealed evidence of diverse rituals and offerings performed at the temple, providing insights into the beliefs and practices of the people who venerated Artemis in Sardis.
subtitle: Grand Temple of Lydian Artemis

keyMyths.title: Foundation of the Temple
keyMyths.description: Legend says the temple was founded on a site sacred to a pre-existing Anatolian mother goddess. The temple's construction was divinely inspired.

keyMyths.title: Artemis' Protection of Sardis
keyMyths.description: Artemis was believed to protect Sardis from enemies and natural disasters. Her blessings ensured prosperity and well-being for the city.

sources.author: Crawford H. Greenewalt, Jr.
sources.work: Sardis: From Prehistoric to Roman Times
sources.description: A comprehensive overview of the history and archaeology of Sardis.
sources.text: A comprehensive overview of the history and archaeology of Sardis.

sources.author: Nicholas Cahill
sources.work: Household and City Organization at Oikoi in Hellenistic Sardis
sources.description: Explores the social and urban contexts of Hellenistic Sardis.
sources.text: Explores the social and urban contexts of Hellenistic Sardis.
